title: Retirement confidence gap leaves many ignoring super
full_text:
One in three Australians approaching retirement are failing to actively manage their superannuation, with uncertainty and low confidence preventing many from preparing for life after work, according to new research from Aware Super.
The research, which surveyed Australians aged 55 to 75 who are not fully retired, found 32 per cent were not taking steps to improve or plan for their retirement.
The findings suggest emotional barriers, rather than apathy, are discouraging engagement, with many respondents overwhelmed by the perceived complexity of retirement planning or convinced they have left it too late to make a difference.
Almost half of those identified as disengaged fell into two broad groups: people who deliberately avoided thinking about retirement and those who believed they had accumulated too little super to justify engaging with it.
While checking account balances remained the most common interaction, a significant number of respondents had not taken any action on their super over the past year.
Aware Super head of guidance Lynda Cross said the research pointed to a confidence problem rather than a lack of interest.
“What this research tells us is that people aren’t ignoring their retirement because they don’t care – it’s because they feel unsure where to start, or worried they’ve already fallen behind,” Cross said. “We’re seeing a lack of confidence stopping people taking even the first step.”
Cross said building financial confidence earlier in life and making retirement planning easier to navigate would help improve outcomes for Australians.
“Even small moments of gradual engagement – like logging in to your account or trying out an online calculator – can shift how people feel about their financial future over time. The earlier people engage, the better their future retirement outcomes tend to be but too many Australians are leaving it until much later, or avoiding it altogether,” she said.
The findings come as super funds increasingly invest in digital guidance tools aimed at encouraging members to engage with retirement planning earlier.
Aware Super said its Retirement Manager service allows eligible members to model retirement income scenarios, estimate how long their savings may last and access guidance from retirement advisers without paying additional fees.
Cross said helping Australians build confidence through simple actions could have a meaningful impact on retirement readiness.
“Helping people take simple, practical steps – and showing that it’s never too early or too late to engage – can make a meaningful difference to how confident they feel heading into retirement.”
author: Adrian Suljanovic
heading: Superannuation and Investments
page_title: Retirement confidence gap leaves many ignoring super - Super Review
